title: Hepatic portal vein and portosystemic anastomosis
body_html:
The hepatic portal vein (portal vein) is a continuation of the splenic vein and superior mesenteric vein. It supplies approximately 75% of hepatic blood flow, the remainder comes from the hepatic artery.

Image sourced from Wikipedia
Diagram illustrating formation of Portal Vein

The primary purpose of the portal vein is to transport nutrients absorbed from the GI tract to the liver to be metabolised. 100% of blood flow from the portal system should drain into the liver but in situations of raised portal pressure (eg in liver cirrhosis) blood will instead flow through sites where portal and systemic venous systems meet. These sites are known as sites of portosystemic anastomosis. Varices may form at these sites as a result of the high pressure blood flow through these vessels.

There are five sites of portosystemic anastomosis:

  • Oesophageal
  • Rectal
  • Intrahepatic
  • Retroperitoneal
  • Paraumbilical.

Image sourced from Wikipedia
Sites of portosystemic anastomosis


Site of Portosystemic anastomosisPortal/Systemic Veins
Lower Oesophageal
  • Portal: left gastric veins
  • Systemic: Azygos vein
Upper Anal Canal
  • Portal: Superior rectal veins
  • Systemic: Middle and Inferior rectal veins
Umbilical
  • Portal: Veins ligamentum teres
  • Systemic: Superior and Inferior Epigastric Veins
Bare Area of Liver
  • Portal: Hepatic and Portal veins
  • Systemic: Inferior Phrenic veins
Retroperitoneal
  • Portal: Colonic veins
  • Body wall veins

In clinical practice the significance of these anastomoses are seen in raised portal pressure i.e. portal hypertension. Varices (particularly oesophageal and rectal) can form and if they rupture result in massive heamorrhage.

Umbilical varicies manifest as caput medusae (see below)

Image sourced from Wikipedia
Diagram illustrating paraumbilical varices (caput medusae)
